context: ER diagram for a concert-tracking domain. Entities: Person (Personal ID, Name, Surname, Birth Date) is a generalization of Audience (Favourite Music Type), Artistic Director (Biography), Maestro (Biography, Stage Name), and Musician (Biography). Instrument(Name, Type e.g. chordophones, Description). Orchestra(Name, Foundation Date, Biography). Track(Title, Duration, Composer, Music Type). Concert(ID, Title, Start Time, Duration). Location(Name, Address, Type, N Seats). Relationships: Audience –Attend(0,N)–(1,N)– Concert; Artistic Director –Organize(1,1)–(1,N)– Concert; Maestro –Direct(1,1)–(1,1)– Orchestra (attribute: First Concert Yes/No); Orchestra –Compose(1,2)–(10,N)– Musician; Orchestra –Perform(0,N)–(1,N)– Track; Concert –At(0,N)–(1,N)– Track; Concert –Located(1,1)–(1,N)– Location; Musician –Play(1,N)–(1,N)– Instrument (attribute: Since).
prompt: Which DB technology would be the best to store the entities/relationships of the ER diagram? Fill in a table with columns ENTITIES/REL., DB TYPE, MOTIVATION. You may not need all the rows of the table.
solution:
A reasonable assignment (several are defensible if well motivated):

| Entities/Rel. | DB type | Motivation |
|---|---|---|
| Audience, Artistic Director, Concert, Location, Attend, Organize | **Graph DB (Neo4j)** | The domain is dominated by many-to-many relationships (who attended which concert, organized by whom, at which location) that need to be traversed and counted efficiently; graph traversals avoid expensive joins. |
| Orchestra, Maestro, Musician, Instrument, Direct, Compose, Play | **Document DB (MongoDB)** | Orchestra/Maestro/Musician/Instrument form a hierarchical, mostly self-contained aggregate (an orchestra with its director and members and the instruments they play) that maps naturally to nested/embedded documents, and access patterns are aggregate-oriented rather than requiring arbitrary graph traversals. |
| Track, At, Perform | **Search engine (Elasticsearch)** or relational | Track metadata (title, composer, music type, duration) is queried with full-text/range filters and relevance ranking (e.g., search by composer, filter by duration), which fits a search index; alternatively a simple relational table if only exact lookups are needed. |

No single NoSQL model fits the whole diagram; the key point is choosing the model whose data-access pattern (graph traversal vs. aggregate retrieval vs. search/ranking) matches each part of the schema.

context: Consider the entities Audience, Concert, Artistic Director, and Location from the ER model described in Exercise 1.
prompt: Suppose you want to store the respective data instances of Audience, Concert, Artistic Director, and Location in a graph database. Sketch a graph model/example describing the nodes, main attributes, and edges. Either show an example graph or a graph with types.
solution:
A graph-with-types model:

```
(:AudienceMember {personalId, name, surname, birthDate, favouriteMusicType})
(:ArtisticDirector {personalId, name, surname, biography})
(:Concert {id, title, startTime, duration})
(:Location {name, address, type, nSeats})

(:AudienceMember)-[:ATTEND]->(:Concert)
(:ArtisticDirector)-[:ORGANIZE]->(:Concert)
(:Concert)-[:LOCATED_AT]->(:Location)
```

Each node type carries the attributes of the corresponding entity; `ATTEND` corresponds to the (0,N)-(1,N) Attend relationship, `ORGANIZE` to the (1,1)-(1,N) Organize relationship (each concert has exactly one organizing director), and `LOCATED_AT` links a concert to the single location where it is held.

context: Graph model from the previous part: (:AudienceMember)-[:ATTEND]->(:Concert), (:ArtisticDirector)-[:ORGANIZE]->(:Concert), (:Concert)-[:LOCATED_AT]->(:Location).
prompt: Write a Cypher query to count the total number of concerts managed by each artistic director whose name is "Chiara", that lasted more than 100 minutes, and with more than 1000 attendances.
solution:
```cypher
MATCH (d:ArtisticDirector {name: "Chiara"})-[:ORGANIZE]->(c:Concert)
WHERE c.duration > 100
MATCH (a:AudienceMember)-[:ATTEND]->(c)
WITH d, c, count(a) AS attendance
WHERE attendance > 1000
RETURN d.personalId AS director, count(c) AS totalConcerts
```

The first `MATCH`/`WHERE` restricts to concerts organized by a director named "Chiara" that lasted more than 100 minutes; the second `MATCH` counts attendees per concert via the `ATTEND` edge; the outer `WHERE` keeps only concerts with more than 1000 attendances; the final `RETURN` aggregates the surviving concerts per director.

context: Graph model: (:AudienceMember)-[:ATTEND]->(:Concert)-[:LOCATED_AT]->(:Location). `now() - birthdate` returns the age of a person.
prompt: Write a Cypher query to collect, for each audience member that attended at least 30 concerts, the amount of different locations where the member participated in at least one concert. Consider only the people who attended at least 500 of such concerts and return their average age (`now() - birthdate` returns the age).
solution:
```cypher
MATCH (a:AudienceMember)-[:ATTEND]->(c:Concert)-[:LOCATED_AT]->(l:Location)
WITH a, count(DISTINCT c) AS numConcerts, count(DISTINCT l) AS numLocations
WHERE numConcerts >= 30
WITH a, numLocations, numConcerts
WHERE numConcerts >= 500
RETURN avg(duration.between(a.birthDate, date()).years) AS averageAge, avg(numLocations) AS avgDistinctLocations
```

The first `WITH`/`WHERE` computes, per audience member, the number of concerts attended and the number of distinct locations visited, keeping only members with at least 30 concerts; the second `WHERE` further restricts to members with at least 500 concerts (a stricter condition that supersedes the first). The final `RETURN` aggregates the average age (`now() - birthdate`, expressed here with `duration.between`) across that filtered set, alongside the average number of distinct locations they visited. Note: the exam text asks for "their average age" as a single aggregate over the qualifying members, which is what this query returns.

context: Consider the entities Orchestra, Maestro, Musician, and Instrument from the ER model in Exercise 1: a Maestro directs exactly one Orchestra (Direct, 1,1-1,1), an Orchestra is composed of 10..N Musicians who may belong to 1..2 orchestras (Compose), and a Musician plays 1..N Instruments (Play, with attribute Since).
prompt: How many collections would you define within a documental database for the entities Orchestra, Maestro, Musician, and Instrument (clearly state it)? How would you implement the relations between the concepts? Provide a simple documental representation.

context: Collection `persons` holds musicians as `{ role: "Musician", name, instruments: [{ instrumentId, name, since }] }`.
prompt: Write a query to compute the total number of musicians who can play the piano and whose name is "Fabiana".
solution:
```javascript
db.persons.countDocuments({
  role: "Musician",
  name: "Fabiana",
  "instruments.name": "Piano"
})
```

Dot-notation `"instruments.name": "Piano"` matches any document whose `instruments` array contains an element with `name` equal to `"Piano"`; combined with the equality filters on `role` and `name`, `countDocuments` returns the count directly.

context: Collection `persons` holds musicians as `{ role: "Musician", orchestraIds: [...], instruments: [{ instrumentId, name, since }] }`.
prompt: Write a query to compute the number of members for each orchestra and each instrument, and return only the orchestras that include at least three musicians for at least four instruments.

context: Collection `orchestras` embeds `director: { name, ... }`; collection `persons` holds musicians as `{ role: "Musician", name, orchestraIds: [...], instruments: [{ name }] }`.
prompt: Write a query to count the number of orchestras directed by directors named "Claudio" and including at least one member named "Lorenzo" who can play the piano.

context: Consider the entity Instrument, to be stored in Redis.
prompt: Create a set of instruments including "Keyboard", "Violin", and "Drums".
solution:
```
SADD instruments Keyboard Violin Drums
```

`SADD` creates the key `instruments` as a Redis **Set** (if it doesn't exist yet) and adds the three members; sets automatically de-duplicate and have no ordering guarantee, which fits an unordered collection of instrument names.

context: Redis set `instruments` created with `SADD instruments Keyboard Violin Drums`.
prompt: Perform an operation to check whether "Flute" is part of the set.
solution:
```
SISMEMBER instruments Flute
```

`SISMEMBER` returns `1` if the member is present in the set and `0` otherwise, in O(1) time.

context: Redis set `instruments` created with `SADD instruments Keyboard Violin Drums`.
prompt: Perform an operation to add "Flute" to the set, with time to live equal to 10.
solution:
```
SADD instruments Flute
EXPIRE instruments 10
```

`SADD` adds "Flute" to the existing set, and `EXPIRE instruments 10` sets a 10-second time-to-live. Note that Redis's native Set type does not support a per-member TTL: `EXPIRE` applies to the whole key `instruments`, so after 10 seconds the **entire set** (not just "Flute") would expire. If a genuinely per-element TTL is required, an alternative is to model each instrument as its own string key (e.g. `SET instrument:Flute 1 EX 10`) instead of a Set member.

context: Consider the Track entity: Title, Duration (minutes), Composer (e.g., Mozart), Music Type (e.g., classical).
prompt: Provide the complete mapping of the index for the Track entity (i.e., field name, field type, the structure of the mapping, etc.).

context: Index `tracks` mapped as: title (text), duration (integer), composer (keyword), musicType (keyword).
prompt: Write a query to return the documents whose composer is "Bach", whose duration is greater than 8 minutes, and whose music type is classical, prioritizing those with duration greater than 11 minutes. The condition on the composer must not affect the final score.
